client_input.title: Anjum Kadari & Anr. vs Union of India & Ors.
client_input.description: The Uttar Pradesh Board of Madarsa Education Act, 2004 has been held unconstitutional by the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad on the grounds that it violates the principle of secularism and Articles 14 and 21A of the Constitution.

citations_found.case_title: Anjum Kadari vs Union Of India on 5 November, 2024
citations_found.summary: Legal Document View ] \ , Cited by \] Supreme Court of India Anjum Kadari vs Union Of India on 5 November, 2024 Author: Bench: ``` Reportable 2024 INSC 831 IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA CIVIL APPELLATE/ ORIGINAL JURISDICTION Special Leave Petition No.8541 of 2024 Anjum Kadari & Anr. …Appellants Versus Union of India & Ors. …Respondents ``` With Special Leave Petition No.7857 of 2024 With Special Leave Petition No.7821 of 2024 With Special Leave Petition No.7878 of 2024 With Special Leave Petitio...
citations_found.ai_summary:
Here's a 2-3 sentence summary of the case:

The dispute in Anjum Kadari vs Union Of India was about the validity of the Madarsa Act, a regulatory legislation governing madarsas (Islamic schools) in India, and whether it violates the principles of secularism and the basic structure of the Constitution. The court ultimately decided that the Madarsa Act is a regulatory legislation that does not violate the principles of secularism or the basic structure of the Constitution.

Judgment Reserved on - 08.02.2024 Judgment delivered on - 22.03.2024 Court No. - 2 Case :- WRIT - C No. - 6049 of 2023 Petitioner :- Anshuman Singh Rathore Respondent :- Union Of India Thr...
citations_found.ai_summary:
Here is a 2-3 sentence summary of the Indian legal case:

The dispute in this case was about the validity of the Madarsa Education Act, 2019, and the regulations made thereunder, which were challenged by various petitioners on grounds of violating secularism, Articles 21 and 21-A of the Indian Constitution, and conflicting with the U.G.C. Act. The Allahabad High Court, in a judgment delivered on March 22, 2024, held that the Madarsa Education Act, 2019, was not violative of the provisions of the Indian Constitution. The court also ruled that the regulations made under the Act were valid.

Here is a 2-3 sentence summary of the Indian legal case:

The dispute in Anjum Kadari vs Union Of India was about the validity of the Madarsa Act, a regulatory legislation governing madarsas (Islamic schools) in the state, with the appellants challenging its constitutionality. The court, led by Justice Dhananjaya Y Chandrachud, ultimately decided that the Madarsa Act is a regulatory legislation that does not violate the basic structure of the Constitution, including the rights of minority educational institutions under Article 30.

citations_found.ai_summary:
Here is a 2-3 sentence summary of the Indian legal case:

The dispute in Anjum Kadari vs Union Of India was about the validity of the Madarsa Act, a regulatory legislation for madarsas (Islamic educational institutions) in the state of Uttar Pradesh, which was challenged by several madarsas and individuals. The court, led by CJI Dhananjaya Y Chandrachud, ultimately decided that the Madarsa Act is a regulatory legislation that does not violate the basic structure of the Constitution, including Article 30, which guarantees the right to establish and administer educational institutions by minorities.

citations_found.ai_summary:
Here is a 2-3 sentence summary of the Indian legal case:

The dispute in Anshuman Singh Rathore vs Union Of India was about the validity of the Madarsa Act and its regulations in the state of Uttar Pradesh, specifically the provision that allows madarsas to be exempt from the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009. The court ultimately decided that the Madarsa Act and its regulations are not violative of Articles 14, 21, and 21-A of the Indian Constitution, and that there is no conflict between the Madarsa Act and the U.G.C. Act.
